resume:
Dr. Helena Vasquez-Ruiz
Vice President of Research & Development | Biopharmaceutical and Diagnostics

Professional Summary:
Accomplished research executive with more than eighteen years of experience leading discovery and translational research organizations across biopharmaceutical and molecular-diagnostics companies. I have built and scaled multidisciplinary R&D functions spanning early discovery, assay development, preclinical pharmacology, translational biomarkers, and regulatory science. Over my career I have been accountable for research portfolios ranging from early exploratory programs through programs entering first-in-human studies, and I have repeatedly been brought in to bring structure, scientific rigor, and decision discipline to organizations that were generating data but struggling to convert it into prioritized, fundable programs. My leadership philosophy centers on three commitments: that scientific decisions should be governed by pre-specified evidence thresholds rather than enthusiasm; that a research portfolio is a set of bets that must be actively pruned, not merely accumulated; and that translational science succeeds only when discovery, clinical, and regulatory functions share a single integrated development hypothesis from the very beginning.

Professional Experience:

Meridian Therapeutics (2019-present) — Vice President, Research & Development
Reporting to the Chief Scientific Officer, I lead an R&D organization of approximately one hundred and forty scientists and staff across discovery biology, medicinal chemistry, translational sciences, bioinformatics, and research operations. I own the research portfolio governance process, the annual research budget, and the scientific go/no-go decisions that move programs from discovery into development. During my tenure I established the company's first formal stage-gate framework for advancing programs, introduced a quantitative target-validation scorecard that every program must clear before significant medicinal-chemistry investment, and restructured the translational sciences group so that biomarker strategy is defined at candidate nomination rather than retrofitted later. I chair the Research Review Committee, I represent R&D to the board's science and technology committee, and I partner closely with Business Development on in-licensing scientific diligence. I have led the organization through two major portfolio re-prioritizations, including one in which we deliberately terminated several long-running but underperforming programs in order to concentrate resources on a smaller number of higher-conviction bets.

Northlight Biosciences (2013-2019) — Senior Director, then Executive Director, Translational Research
I joined to build a translational research capability that the company lacked and grew it from a small team into a fully staffed function spanning biomarker discovery, assay validation, and clinical-sample operations. I designed the biomarker strategy for the lead oncology program, established the company's relationships with central laboratories and academic collaborators, and built the data-management practices that allowed translational results to feed directly into clinical-development decisions. I was promoted to Executive Director after leading the translational work that supported a key program's transition into clinical studies, and in that expanded role I took on responsibility for research informatics and for the scientific governance of external research collaborations.

Caldera Diagnostics (2007-2013) — Principal Scientist, then Group Leader, Assay Development
I began my industry career in molecular-diagnostics assay development, where I led a group responsible for designing, validating, and transferring nucleic-acid and immunoassay panels from research into the regulated development pipeline. I was accountable for analytical validation strategy, for the scientific content of design-control documentation, and for the technical interface with quality and regulatory functions. This early grounding in regulated assay development shaped my conviction that scientific rigor and documentation discipline are not in tension with speed but are in fact what make speed sustainable.

Selected Leadership Themes:
Throughout my career I have repeatedly been the person asked to impose decision discipline on research organizations rich in talent and data but poor in prioritization. I have consistently championed pre-specified evidence thresholds, active portfolio pruning, and the early integration of discovery, translational, and regulatory thinking. I am equally comfortable in a scientific review defending the mechanistic basis of a program and in a boardroom explaining why we are choosing to kill a beloved project. I believe that the hardest and most valuable thing a research leader does is say no early, clearly, and on the basis of evidence.

Approach to Portfolio Governance and Scientific Decision-Making:
The operating system I bring to a research organization rests on a small number of mechanisms that I have refined across three companies. First, every program carries an explicit, pre-registered evidence hypothesis: before we commit meaningful chemistry or biology resource, the team must write down what would have to be true for the program to succeed and what observation would cause us to stop. Second, I run a standing portfolio review on a fixed cadence in which every active program is scored against the same target-validation and translatability criteria, so that funding is a continuous decision rather than an annual entitlement; programs do not coast, and a program that was funded last quarter must re-earn its resource this quarter. Third, I deliberately separate the scientist who champions a program from the reviewer who scores its evidence, because I have seen too many organizations confuse conviction with validation. Fourth, I insist that translational and regulatory colleagues are in the room at candidate nomination, not invited late, so that the development hypothesis is integrated rather than stitched together after the fact. I have found that these mechanisms, applied consistently, do more to accelerate good programs than any single piece of cleverness, because they free capacity from programs that should have been stopped and concentrate it where the biology is strongest.

External Engagements and Scientific Community:
Beyond my line responsibilities I have served on scientific advisory boards for early-stage diagnostics and therapeutics ventures, acted as a reviewer for several peer-reviewed journals in molecular and translational biology, and mentored a number of scientists who have gone on to lead their own discovery functions. I have spoken at industry and academic venues on the discipline of portfolio pruning and on the integration of translational science into early development, and I view this external engagement as a way of keeping my own thinking honest and current rather than as a distraction from operating responsibility.

Education and Affiliations:
Ph.D., Molecular Biology, University of Edinburgh
B.Sc., Biochemistry, University of Barcelona
Postdoctoral training in translational oncology
Member, several professional research and translational-medicine societies; frequent reviewer for peer-reviewed journals; occasional advisor to early-stage life-science ventures
jd: Meridian Therapeutics seeks a Vice President of Research & Development to lead our discovery and translational research organization through a period of portfolio focus and pipeline maturation. Reporting to the Chief Scientific Officer, you will own research portfolio governance, the stage-gate framework that moves programs from discovery into development, and the scientific go/no-go decisions that determine where the organization invests. You will be accountable for the quality and pace of scientific decision-making, for the integration of discovery, translational, and regulatory strategy, and for the active pruning of the portfolio so that resources concentrate on the highest-conviction programs. We are looking for a leader who can narrate a hard scientific decision with full rigor: the situation the portfolio was in, the specific evidence threshold that was or was not met, the actions taken and who owned them, and the measured consequences over time. Strong candidates tell complete, evidence-anchored stories — situation, task, the specific actions they personally drove, and the quantified result — and can defend a kill decision as confidently as a go decision.

history.q: Give me the scope of your R&D org at Meridian — functions, headcount, and the decisions you personally own.
You established the company's first stage-gate framework. What was decision-making like before it existed?
What specifically triggered you to build the stage-gate — was there a precipitating program failure?
Tell me about the target-validation scorecard. What does a program have to clear, concretely?
Who designed that scorecard — you, a committee, or did you adapt it from somewhere?
Let's go to the portfolio re-prioritization where you terminated programs. How many programs, and what was the portfolio size?
What was the situation that forced that re-prioritization — budget, strategy, or data?
Walk me through the single hardest program you chose to kill. What was it?
What specific evidence threshold did that program fail to meet?
Who actually made the kill call — you alone, the Research Review Committee, the CSO?
What did you personally do to get the organization aligned behind killing a program people loved?
How much budget or headcount did terminating it free up, and where did it go?
Over what timeframe did you see the benefit of concentrating those resources?
Did any of the surviving programs measurably advance faster because of the reallocation?
Looking back, was killing that program the right call? How do you know?
What would you do differently in how you ran that re-prioritization?
Give me the whole story of that hardest kill decision, start to finish, with the evidence and the numbers.

candidate_last_answer: Here's the full arc. Situation: when I arrived, our flagship discovery program — a kinase inhibitor that the founders were emotionally invested in — had consumed roughly forty percent of discovery chemistry capacity for almost three years, but its target-validation package was thin: the genetic-knockdown phenotype was modest and we had no clean evidence the target was driving the disease biology in human tissue. Task: as I rolled out the stage-gate, the CSO asked me to apply the new target-validation scorecard to the existing portfolio retroactively, including this program. Action: I personally re-ran the validation review, commissioned an independent human-tissue expression study and a CRISPR knockout in two disease-relevant models, and set a pre-specified threshold — the program had to show a defined effect size in at least one human-relevant model to stay funded. The knockout came back essentially null. I presented the package to the Research Review Committee, recommended termination, and I personally sat down with the two lead scientists before the meeting so they heard it from me first and helped shape the wind-down. Result: we terminated it, which freed roughly eighteen full-time chemists and about two and a half million dollars of annual spend, and we redirected that capacity to two earlier programs with stronger validation. Within about fourteen months one of those advanced to candidate nomination, roughly two quarters ahead of its original plan. Was it right? Yes — eighteen months later a competitor's compound against the same target failed in the clinic for exactly the efficacy reason our knockout had predicted. What I'd do differently: I waited almost a full quarter to commission the knockout because I wanted buy-in first, and in hindsight I should have run the decisive experiment immediately and brought the result to the buy-in conversation, not the other way around.

top_question_traits: must-anchor-on: the program had to show a defined effect size in at least one human-relevant model to stay funded

expected-yield: pressure-test the threshold's design rather than its outcome — ask what she would have done if the knockout had shown a borderline-positive effect just under the pre-specified bar, whether the single-model threshold risked killing a true target on one assay's failure, and how she guarded against a false-negative kill, revealing whether her decision discipline is genuinely reasoned about its own error modes or only looks rigorous because the result happened to confirm it
